Alright, JB in the house. Time for your daily motivation slash skills training. Guess what it is? Throwback Thursday. Gonna find a great clip for you. I found some. I had so many but this is one of those I had so many I didn't know what you should choose. I found an awesome one directly related to this idea of bifurcated focus. Alright, and I really dig into this in a really profound way. So let's cut to that right now. Bifurcated focus and I'll see you on the other side. What I want to talk about today is something really, really important and that is the power of what I referred to as bifurcated focus. Now it's intergame competency but it's so crucial to the success in the outer game that it almost like crosses over. Let me explain to you, you know, for those of you who've heard me say this before, many of you have, let me just reiterate what that is and dig a bit deeper here. So bifurcated focus means that you want to focus on your vision for the future with absolute laser guided position. You want to focus on a laser guided position but you don't want to have tunnel vision. In other words, your vision for the future is a living, breathing, organism, a being into itself that grows and changes and it follows and mutates. It doesn't get thrown out the window and you alter your vision. It doesn't like, you don't throw the baby out with the bath water. You modify it, you let it grow, you let it mutate. It's constantly doing that, right? And the way that happens is by focusing on it with laser guided position but with one eye, you're constantly scanning the horizon for other opportunities that come up along the way. In other words, you don't want to have tunnel vision where you're focusing with both eyes with laser guided position and even if a great opportunity comes up, you're like, oh no, no, it's not part of my vision for the future. I can't do that. I'm just like, you're so, you know, you're not flexible and so much about success in business, especially when you're small and you're growing fast, is the ability to pivot. In other words, the big advantage, a small business owner, entrepreneur has, is that they can move very quickly. When they see something, if they like it or an opportunity, bam, they move, there's no red tape to cut through, there's not 18 layers of management that will prove on it, you can see it, act on it and bam, get the result. This is why you find over the long term, it's very difficult for these very large companies to stay on top in the absence of a very effective management thing that allows their underlings to make quick decisions. So you'll notice some of these big companies that are really well run, like at Amazon or Facebook, there's a lot of going on there, like they're giving people on the lower rungs the ability to make decisions because it's almost like, I think the metaphor used in the past was like how the Titanic crashed into the iceberg, right? So remember the movie, the Titanic, these two guys are up top and they're the ice spotters and they're in their crow's nest way up top, right? And they see the iceberg, you know, maybe a half a mile up ahead, they start, oh my God, ice, they ring the bell, they call on the radio on the telephone downstairs to the bridge and they say, iceberg, get ahead. And then the guy at the engine immediately goes iceberg, get ahead and they flip the wheel, they spin the wheel hard to starboard, right? And at the same time, they stick into the engine room and the engine puts the propellers into reverse, right? To essentially port around or starboard around the iceberg. But what happens? The ship keeps going. And the guys on top saying, why aren't they turning? Why aren't they turning? I don't understand. Well, guess what? They were turning, but a large object in motion doesn't turn on a dime. It can't pivot quickly. Now, conversely, if the Titanic were the size of a speedboat and they ran the wheel to the left, to flip to the left and then put the engine in reverse, guess what? They would have went around the iceberg without a problem. But large lumbering ships, even when you spin the wheel, they don't turn quickly. Metaphor here is obvious. If you're in a small entrepreneurial business and running it with an entrepreneurial mindset, then you make decisions and those decisions have immediate impact. It's not like you decide something. When they make a decision at IBM or Microsoft, all right, one of these huge companies, it might take a year to make its way through all the various layers of management and then to be felt throughout the entire institution because it's so large. It's a lumbering giant versus the nimble little companies that can turn and dodge and weave and bam, that's how they displaced the lumbering giants. Now, in your own life as a salesperson, as a business, on whichever one you are, it doesn't matter. You can use this with deadly effectiveness to grow your business, to grow your pipeline. How? By testing out ideas and then immediately, if they work, you pivot, you keep changing and act and bam, you pivot, you pivot, you pivot. Don't just stay set in your ways. On some level, complacency is the enemy of greatness. To be complacent, to think that you're doing good enough, that you're, I never stop testing. I never say to myself, my results are good enough. I'm constantly searching for the next way to improve it. And I want you to do the exact same thing. You're constantly improving your script. You're constantly trying to search for a new lead source. You're testing out different ideas. Now, remember, part of failing elegantly in the flip side, you don't just throw out the old idea, you test. So you don't just like, just discard something before you know it's going to work. But you want to make sure though, that you don't just, you know, say, I'm doing really good and this is how we do business and just that's how things are done. Here's my vision for the future. That's who I am. That's what I do. And that's what you're stuck at. That's not a recipe for long term success. It's just simply not. The bottom line is you have to be constantly on the lookout for new opportunities, new possibilities, new ways of doing business, new ways to get more leads, to be in front of more people. Don't ever settle for what you have. Complacency, seriously. It's like it's a death now. Now, I'm not trying to say here, let me just be honest. I'm not trying to say that you can never enjoy happiness and feel like you're doing great. Yes, of course, you want to enjoy your success best in the glow of great conversions and great sales. Yes, that's there. It's in your heart and in your mind. But that is not mutually exclusive from continuing to improve. You understand? As soon as you get like sort of fat, dumb and happy, so to speak, right? Is the day that you start losing your edge. Now, how does that relate to like vision? Well, the point is I'm bringing it from big picture down to small big picture means that when you have a vision for your future, and that vision includes your business, your methods of doing business, what that does for you, what it brings to your family, where you live, where you vacation, what your life looks like on all levels, right? Then down to the very small, which is your actual day to day, you know, results of how you do business. How do you get to that brass ring in the edge of the vision is like that long term, you know, outcome, then you have these daily and weekly and monthly goals, right? And they integrate together. My point is this, is that you can't in the same way that you're constantly testing ideas day to day and, you know, and making your goals malleable, making sure that you change up, you test new ideas. The same thing goes with your vision, meaning over the long term, you can't just be, you know, say, I came up with an idea and I'm going to have my, this is like for me, I can't just say, I have this great vision. My platform is going to have all these different trainers on it. It'll just be this unbelievable resource for salespeople all over the world. There will be millions of salespeople pay me, you know, between, you know, 1495 a month, whatever it might be right at the time. And I use that money to buy more trainers and speakers. I don't raise the price to my people. I buy, I come up with a new software products that people can use to make money as salespeople. I just create this amazing platform and it grows and grows and grows and gets bought out by Fox News or Amazon, the billions, right? That's a great vision and really honestly, if I share with you in every little nuance of it, it makes so much sense because I'm giving massive value. The whole thing makes sense, right? I make money out of it, especially over the long term, even though it's a bit painful now because I'm reinvesting everything, but here's the thing. If you think that that's my end game, that's where I'm going to end up, you know, the chances are very slim that that's how all things end up. That's my vision. I am constantly authoring it, changing it, growing it, making it better, more powerful and more real to me. The day I stop looking for different ideas to turn my vision into a reality is the day I stick a knife in my own back and like self-destruct. Seriously, don't be that guy. Don't be that girl. Don't just settle for what you have and since my idea is my idea, that's how I do business and that's who I am. Either you're growing or you're dying, either you're changing or you're dying, either you're evolving or you're dying. You get it? There is no stagnation. That's the way of the world, not just in business in all aspects of life. There's no such thing as static equilibrium. You're always staying in the dynamic, dynamic, meaning that while you always are hovering around the same point, like let's say your temperature is 98.6, you're not there all day long. That's your average mean temperature, 98.6 degrees, right? That's a body temperature in Fahrenheit. At any given moment, you're a half a degree above, below and your body regulates around that temperature. That's called dynamic equilibrium. Your success, your vision, it's all dynamic. It's changing, it's growing, you're evolving, you're not static. Static creatures don't last very long. Okay? Think of it like that. I want you to take this. I want you to make today, besides you, so you're going to go out and you're going to kick ass, work your ass off, but I want you to think today about being flexible, about remembering that where you end up, where you ultimately end up, it's not going to be exactly where you think you're going to end up. Where you think you're going to end up, your vision serves as your target that grows, it changes, it evolves, it gets better. So when you see an opportunity that makes sense, that you think can enhance your outcome, enhance your vision, you don't throw out your vision. No, you integrate this opportunity into your vision. You get it? And again, chunking it down to the salesperson day to day, a new lead source, a new way of closing, you'll throw out the all way, you integrate it, you grow, you make it strong, you evolve. That's the key to success. Don't ever stop doing that. Don't ever stop testing, don't ever stop growing, don't ever stop evolving. I promise you that once you cultivate this art of being flexible, of evolving your vision, of bifurcated focus, of keeping that, you know, you're focused on that outcome, boy, you're focused on that long term outcome, with all your emotional heart is in there, but you never stop scanning the horizon for the other opportunities along the way, and it chunks right down to every single day. All right, powerful stuff, right? Remember, just remember, seldom is the company or the individual that got rich in the way they originally thought they would get rich. It's very rare to be a successful entrepreneur, to be a successful visionary, you have to master the art of pivoting. And to pivot, you must have bifurcated focus to know where to pivot. You pivot when you see these things happening around you, integrate into that vision, and you're done. All right, we'll talk again tomorrow. Have a great day.
